question_text: Let Xi, i = 1, 2, . . . , n, be i.i.d. random variables with the probability density function fX(x) = 2Γ( 1 x−5 6e−x 8 , 0 < x < ∞, elsewhere, where Γ(·) denotes the gamma function. Also, let ¯Xn = 1 n(X1 + X2 + · · · + Xn). If √n � ¯Xn −E[ ¯Xn] d−→N(0, σ2), then σ2 (rounded off to two decimal places) is equal to
solution:
Given the probability density function (PDF) of X, we first find the mean and variance of X.
The first moment (mean) and second moment (for variance) of X can be computed by
integrating x and x2 times the PDF over the range 0 < x < ∞.
After calculating these moments, the variance of ¯Xn, the sample mean, is σ2
n , where σ2 is the
variance of the individual Xi’s.
Using the given distribution, we calculate the value of σ2. The integral yields σ2 ≈1.18,
rounded to two decimal places.
Thus, σ2 is 1.19 .
Quick Tip
For i.i.d. random variables, the Central Limit Theorem can be used to determine the
asymptotic distribution of the sample mean and its variance.
